title: LOCAL Act
plain_summary: The LOCAL Act says that the Bureau of Land Management must keep its main office in Grand Junction, Colorado. The bill also says that all the workers who currently work there have to stay in their jobs. Additionally, the government must do a study to see if moving more federal workers who manage public lands to Colorado and other western states would help take better care of the land and help local communities. This law is meant to make sure important land management decisions are made by people who live near the lands they are managing.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Natural Resources.
subjects: Government employee pay, benefits, personnel management

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Local Opportunities, Conservation, and American Lands Act or the LOCAL Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Definitions
sections.text: In this law, Secretary means the Secretary of the Interior (the person who runs the government department that manages federal lands). Western State means any of these states: Alaska, Arizona, California, Colorado, Idaho, Montana, Nevada, New Mexico, Oregon, Utah, Washington, or Wyoming.

sections.title: Section 3 Headquarters Office of the Bureau of Land Management in Grand Junction, Colorado
sections.text: The main office for the Bureau of Land Management (the government agency that manages public lands) must be located in Grand Junction, Colorado. All Bureau of Land Management workers who are stationed in Grand Junction, Colorado when this law is passed must stay stationed in Grand Junction, Colorado.

sections.title: Section 4 Study of Relocation of Existing Bureau of Land Management Employee Positions
sections.text: The Secretary must do a study to see if it makes sense to move more Bureau of Land Management workers to the main office in Grand Junction, Colorado or to other western states that the Secretary chooses. The study must look at whether moving these workers would help manage federal lands better, improve working with local communities, and strengthen tourism, conservation, outdoor activities, livestock grazing, responsible energy production, or other uses of the land. The Secretary must give a report about this study to the House Committee on Natural Resources and the Senate Committee on Energy and Natural Resources within three hundred sixty-five days after this law is passed.
